1: Hypomimnēske autous archais exousiais hypotassesthai, peitharchein (remind them to be subject to rulers and authorities, to obey): hypomimnēskō (to remind by bringing to mind) — the present imperative of continuous reminder; the congregation needs ongoing recall, not once-for-all instruction. Archai kai exousiai (rulers and authorities) — the standard Pauline designation for governing powers (Romans 13:1-7; 1 Peter 2:13-14). Peitharchein (to obey authority/submit to governing power) — the compound of peithō (persuade/trust) + archē (rule/origin); to be persuaded by and submit to legitimate governance. Pros pan ergon agathon etoimous einai (ready for every good work) — civic engagement and public virtue are the Christian's response to the governing order.
2: Mēdena blasphēmein, amachous einai, epieikeis (to slander no one, to be peaceable, gentle): amachos (not fighting, peaceable, non-combative) — a- + mache (battle/fight); the peaceable person who does not enter verbal or physical combat. Epieikēs (gentle, fair, reasonable) — the Aristotelian virtue of epieikeia: the capacity to go beyond strict legal right in favor of what is fair and humane; the "sweet reasonableness" (Matthew Arnold) that characterizes the mature person. Pasan endeiknumenous prautēta pros pantas anthrōpous (showing all gentleness toward all people) — the universality is absolute: not just toward believers or toward social equals but toward everyone.
3: Hēmen gar pote kai hēmeis anoētoi, apeitheis, planōmenoi, douleuontes epithumiais kai hēdonais poikilais (for we too were once foolish, disobedient, deceived, enslaved to various desires and pleasures): the autobiographical grounding of the social ethics — the community's own history of moral failure establishes why they cannot treat outsiders with contempt. Anoētoi (foolish, without understanding) — a- + noeō (to understand); lacking the moral perception to see reality clearly. Douleuontes (enslaved/serving as slaves) — the involuntary servitude to desire; not chosen but captured. En kakia kai phthonō diagontes (spending our time in malice and envy) — the social poison of envious malice that breeds mutual destruction.
4: Hote de hē chrēstotēs kai hē philanthrōpia epephanē tou sōtēros hēmōn theou (but when the kindness and the love-of-humanity of our Savior God appeared): the de (but) marks the salvation-historical turning point; the "once" of Titus 3:3 gives way to the "but when" of divine intervention. Chrēstotēs (kindness, goodness, graciousness) — a warm relational quality: the disposition to give good things generously. Philanthrōpia (love of humanity) — the term from which "philanthropy" derives; in Hellenistic usage it described the benevolent regard of gods and rulers for those below them; its application to God upends the power calculus: the divine Sovereign loves humanity with the warm affection of the patron. Epephanē (appeared) — the third epiphaneia in Titus (Titus 2:11; Titus 3:4) marks the letter's Christological signature.
5: Ouk ex ergōn tōn en dikaiosynē ha epoiēsamen hēmeis alla kata to autou eleos esōsen hēmas (not from works of righteousness that we did but according to his own mercy he saved us): the anti-works-righteousness formula closely parallels Ephesians 2:8-9 and Romans 3:24; the ground of salvation is exclusively divine mercy, not human performance. Dia loutrou palingenesias kai anakainōseōs pneumatos hagiou (through the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Spirit): loutron (washing, bath, laver) — almost certainly baptism; the visible water-rite is the instrument through which regeneration (palingenesia) and renewal (anakainōsis) are worked by the Spirit. Palingenesia (regeneration, new birth) appears only here and Matthew 19:28 (cosmic renewal at the eschaton) in the NT — a remarkable weight for a single term. The Spirit is the agent of both the washing's inner reality and its continuing effect.
6: Hou execheen eph' hēmas plousiōs dia Iēsou Christou tou sōtēros hēmōn (whom he poured out on us richly through Jesus Christ our Savior): ekcheo (to pour out) — the Pentecost vocabulary (Acts 2:17-18, 33, citing Joel 2:28-29; "I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh"); the Spirit poured out generously (plousiōs, richly/abundantly) connects the ongoing experience of the Spirit with the Pentecost event. Dia Iēsou Christou (through Jesus Christ) — Christ as the mediator of the Spirit's outpouring; the Spirit is poured out by the Father through the Son (cf. John 15:26; Titus 16:7).
7: Hina dikaiōthentes tē ekeinon chariti klēronomoi genēthōmen kat' elpida zōēs aiōniou (so that having been justified by his grace we might become heirs according to the hope of eternal life): the salvation-history sequence from Titus 3:4-6 reaches its telos in justification (dikaiōthentes) and inheritance (klēronomoi). The aorist passive dikaiōthentes (having been justified) grounds the inheritance in a prior, completed divine act. Klēronomoi (heirs) — the adoption/inheritance language of Romans 8:17 (co-heirs with Christ); the justified person enters the family and inherits the family's estate. Kat' elpida zōēs aiōniou (according to the hope of eternal life) — the inheritance is presently held in hope, not yet fully received (Romans 8:24-25).
8: Pistos ho logos (faithful/trustworthy is the saying): the fourth Pastoral Epistle formula introducing or endorsing a theological statement (1 Timothy 1:15; Titus 3:1; Titus 4:9; 2 Timothy 2:11). Here it endorses the soteriology of Titus 3:4-7. Peri toutōn boulomei se diabebaiousthai, hina phrontizōsin kalōn ergōn proistasthai hoi pepisteukotes theō (about these things I want you to insist confidently, so that those who have trusted God may be devoted to good works) — proistēmi (to stand before, to lead, to devote oneself to) applied to good works: the justified person who is rich in good works. Theology grounds ethics: the right understanding of grace and justification produces the energy for good works rather than undermining it.
9: Mōras de zētēseis kai genealogias kai ereis kai machas nomikais periistaso (but avoid foolish controversies and genealogies and quarrels and legal disputes): the four nouns build from speculative (zētēseis, controversies/inquiries) through genealogical (genealogiai) to combative (ereis, quarrels) to specifically legal (machai nomikai, legal disputes) — a progression from the merely futile to the genuinely destructive. Periistaso (avoid, stand around, shun) — the same avoidance verb as 2 Timothy 2:16. Eisin gar anōpheleis kai mataioi (for they are unprofitable and futile) — the practical criterion: these debates produce no benefit and are empty of content.
10: Hairetikon anthrōpon meta mian kai deuteran nouthesian paraitou (a divisive/heretical person, after a first and second warning, reject): hairetikos — from haireō (to choose, select); one who creates a faction by selecting their own path away from the community. The word will later develop into "heretic" (one who holds false doctrine), but here the primary sense is the party-maker, the schismatic who divides. Nouthesia (warning, admonition) — nous (mind) + tithēmi (place); to place something in the mind; to admonish. The two-warning rule before exclusion allows for repentance while maintaining community integrity.
11: Eidōs hoti exestraptai ho toioutos kai hamartanei, ōn autokatakritos (knowing that such a person is perverted and sinning, being self-condemned): exstrephō (to turn inside-out, to pervert, to corrupt completely) — the prefix ex- intensifies the turning; the divisive person has been thoroughly corrupted. Autokatakritos (self-condemned) — a hapax in the NT; the condemnation is not imposed from outside but self-generated: the divisive person's own actions constitute the judgment against them. This limits the community's role: they are not pronouncing a new verdict but acknowledging the verdict the person's own conduct has already declared.
12: Hotan pempsō Arteman pros se ē Tychikon, speudason elthein pros me eis Nikopolin (when I send Artemas or Tychicus to you, make haste to come to me at Nicopolis): Artemas is otherwise unknown; Tychicus is Paul's trusted emissary (Ephesians 6:21; Colossians 4:7; 2 Timothy 4:12; Acts 20:4). The sending of a replacement allows Titus to leave Crete. Nicopolis (Victory City) — most likely Nicopolis in Epirus (northwest Greece, modern Preveza), founded by Augustus to commemorate his victory at Actium (31 BCE). It was a significant Roman colony on the Adriatic route. Ekei gar kekrika paracheimasai (for there I have decided to spend the winter) — Paul's travel plans grounding the letter in a specific historical moment.
13: Zēnan ton nomikon kai Apollō spoudaiōs propempson (Zenas the lawyer and Apollos send on their way diligently): Zenas is otherwise unknown — nomikos may mean a Jewish Torah-expert (the standard NT use) or a Roman jurist; both interpretations have ancient support. Apollos appears in Acts 18:24-28, 1 Corinthians 1-4, and 16:12 as a significant Alexandrian teacher; his presence in the Pastoral Epistles is notable. They are apparently the carriers of the letter to Titus. Hina mēden autois leipē (so that they lack nothing) — the community's responsibility to supply traveling missionaries (cf. 3 John 5-8; Romans 15:24).
14: Manthanetōsan de kai hoi hēmeteroi kalōn ergōn proistasthai eis tas anagkaias chreias, hina mē ōsin akarpoi (let our people also learn to devote themselves to good works for urgent needs, so that they may not be unfruitful): the final practical instruction of the letter; kalōn ergōn proistasthai (to devote themselves to good works) repeats the phrase from Titus 3:8. Anagkaias chreias (urgent/pressing needs) — the practical needs that the community must address through active good-work investment. Akarpoi (unfruitful) — the fruitlessness of faith that does not express itself in good works; James 2:17 makes the same point. The letter that opened with the apostolic mandate ends with the community's responsibility for practical fruitfulness.
15: Aspazontai se hoi met' emou pantes (all those with me greet you): the anonymous group around Paul in his current location (Nicopolis, or wherever he was at the letter's writing). Aspasai tous philountas hēmas en pistei (greet those who love us in the faith) — the community of mutual love within the faith; the recipients are distinguished by love. Hē charis meta pantōn hymōn (grace be with all of you) — the plural hymōn confirms, as in 1 Timothy 6:21 and 2 Timothy 4:22, that behind the individual addressee stands a congregation. The letter closes with the grace that opened salvation-history in Titus 2:11 — grace appeared (epephanē) and grace concludes the letter.
